name: Hubba Hubba
access: Icicle Road off US-2 near Leavenworth, approximately 6-8 miles up canyon. Popular beginner ice climb.
watch_out: Avalanche terrain: Multiple converging avalanche gullies with 30-45 degree slopes. Frequent avalanches on approach and descent routes.
Soft slab avalanche potential: observed avalanche activity between Doctor Creek and Victoria Creek at 5,500 ft on north-facing aspects
Ice quality highly variable: Thin and rotten ice common in transition zones; hollow ice in lower sections; water-spitting holes; melting pillars and detached ice during warm conditions
North-eastern aspect provides late-season ice preservation - best conditions January-February; susceptible to rapid deterioration during warm periods
Temperature-driven transitions from dry to moist snow (moist below 3,500 ft, dry above) - affects both ice quality and avalanche stability
Wind-loaded slopes above: Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm at 4,500 ft) creates hidden instability; variable bonding makes slope stability assessment critical
Rappel descent - verify anchor quality on ice; be aware of loose rock and ice debris below major flows
Route stays in condition longer than southern-facing alternatives due to orientation - check recent Mountain Project trip reports before approach
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

name: Center Flow
access: Hubba Hubba area, Icicle Creek Road. Large flow when conditions are good.
watch_out: Water ice dependent on flow - varies by year and winter precipitation patterns; flow can be large when in good shape
Exposed aspect to temperature swings causing freeze-thaw cycles
Adjacent avalanche terrain: soft slab terrain at 5,000-5,500 ft elevation
Two-pitch climb with ledge between pitches - belay stances have bolted anchors on left (rock wall on right side alternative)
Descent hazard: two-rappel descent requires careful anchor assessment
Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm deep up to 4,500 ft) creates hidden instability in surrounding terrain
Route condition highly variable - check recent Mountain Project trip reports before approach
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

name: Left of Hubba Hubba
access: Hubba Hubba area, Icicle Creek Road. Small flow to the left of main Hubba Hubba flow. Can be 1-2 pitches depending on conditions.
watch_out: Thin ice on upper pitch - WI2-3 conditions on slab section requiring precise ice tool placement
Lower angle slab ice more susceptible to freeze-thaw breakage than steep flows
Avalanche exposure: north-facing background slopes at ~5,500 ft - monitor wind slab development
Serac/icefall hazard: adjacent waterfalls above route can shed ice - time ascent to avoid peak melt periods (early afternoon)
Moist snow transition (below 3,500 ft) affects base conditions and descent safety
Belay ledge ice quality varies significantly - variable ice conditions require pre-trip assessment
Two-pitch descent requires good route-finding in winter conditions
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

name: Buttress Left
access: Eightmile Buttress area, Icicle Creek Road (mile marker 6.4). GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518. Pitch 1: climb left-running ramp with belay on exposed rock. Pitch 2: traverse right across buttress to WI3 ice below roof, top out onto steep snow.
watch_out: Mixed climbing (M2) requires rock tool placement on ice-covered granite - unpredictable rock ice adherence
Transitional climbing terrain between ice and rock creates anchor placement difficulty
Avalanche terrain: Eightmile Buttress faces east/north - morning ascents recommended to avoid afternoon warming
Soft slab avalanche risk from wind-loaded slopes above (Christmas crust layer creates weak substrate)
Serac hazard from icefall flows above - monitor for rockfall/ice debris
GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518 - area shows recent avalanche activity in NWAC observations
Descent: traverse climber's left, rappel with two 70m ropes to P1 webbing anchor. Alternative: bolt anchors on exposed rock (right), or possibly walk-off upper and far right
Two-pitch route - sustained commitment with limited escape options once committed
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting

name: Icicle Buttress
access: Icicle Creek Road, mile marker 6.4, elevation 2,090 ft (637 m). GPS: 47.54823, -120.7518. Multiple ice and mixed lines on buttress: left, middle, and right sides.
watch_out: Highly variable conditions - left side (lower angle slab, often thin) vs right side (steeper WI4 ice, sustained) vs middle (occasional steeper sections)
Left side: thinner ice on slab requiring delicate footwork and ice tool precision; susceptible to freeze-thaw degradation; WI2-WI3 upper pitch
Right side: steeper ice (WI4) with sustained climbing - higher exposure to falling ice and serac hazard
Middle section: occasionally steeper ice appears creating unexpected grade surprises and protection challenges
Water ice formation depends on flow - highly season and year-dependent; poor formation in low-precipitation winters
Rock gear necessary for mixed sections - M2-M4 climbing possible on steeper sections with rock/ice transitions
Avalanche terrain: entire buttress area exposed to soft slab hazard from north-facing slopes above
Serac/icefall: upper sections have active ice flows that can shed large blocks - time climbing to avoid afternoon melt
Wind-loaded slopes above (confirmed Christmas crust layer up to 4,500 ft) create ongoing instability
Limited screw placements on thin sections - may require unconventional anchor rigging
Multiple pitch descent routes - route-finding required in winter whiteout conditions
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall 1/2-mile buffer closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting
High commitment three-pitch climb with serious consequence if retreat becomes necessary

name: Eightmile Buttress Ice (General)
access: Eightmile area, Icicle Creek Road, approximately 8 miles up canyon from highway 2
watch_out: Multiple unnamed lines with highly variable conditions - requires current beta from local climbers
Elevation range 3,500-5,500 ft - transition zone between moist and dry snow affecting both ice quality and avalanche character
Avalanche terrain: east/north-facing slopes above buttress show documented soft slab activity
Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm deep up to 4,500 ft) creates hidden layer instability - assess slope sensitivity before committing to approach
Waterfall ice sensitive to flow variations - check current water conditions before trip
Serac/icefall hazard: multiple active ice formations above routes - descend early to avoid afternoon ice fall
Rapid temperature swings (freezing rain crust documented up to 4,500 ft) create dangerous freeze-thaw cycles
Isolated cracking and small loose avalanches reported - human-triggered soft slabs likely on steep terrain
Route-finding hazard: winter whitout conditions combined with multiple unnamed lines increase descent complexity
Access issue: road conditions variable through end of 2026; verify trailhead access before traveling

name: Colchuck Lake approaches (Alpine Ice)
access: Eightmile Road, 3.8 miles from fork to trailhead at 3,400 ft. Colchuck Lake hike gains 1,100 ft to glacier approaches
watch_out: Crevasse hazard: glaciated terrain on approach to Colchuck Col and Dragontail - no documented bridges but crevasses present
Hidden snow bridges: early season snow can conceal waterfall holes and crevasse entries - rope team recommended
Glacier ice can develop icy conditions by mid-season - ice tool and crampons essential
Avalanche exposure: north-facing slopes (Colchuck Col approach) susceptible to soft slab hazard
Serac/icefall: glacier calving on upper sections - monitor for falling ice blocks during ascent
Mixed terrain: Class 3-4 scrambling mixed with ice sections - requires ability to rope up quickly
Descent hazard: downclimbing on steep snow and ice with limited anchor options - fall consequence severe
Seasonal window tight: best conditions late season (March-May) when consolidated but before heavy melt
Road access: Eightmile Road closed or difficult through winter - verify current conditions
Winter route-finding: whiteout conditions eliminate landmarks - map/compass/GPS navigation essential
Cold temperature inversion: moist snow below 3,500 ft transitions to dry above - assess stability carefully

name: Millennium Wall
access: Icicle Creek area (exact location requires local beta). Training and development area with reliable ice.
watch_out: Running water on frozen waterfalls - active water flow affects ice quality and creates dangerous thin sections
Difficult route-finding in winter conditions - blazes and landmarks obscured by snow
Creek crossings on approach - stream crossings during high water periods can be dangerous
Thin ice requiring unconventional anchor rigging - partial penetration screws may require creative solutions
South-facing exposure: sun-driven melting limits climbing windows to morning/early afternoon
Avalanche terrain: approach slopes can slide during warming periods
Descent hazards: downclimbing potential if unable to rappel from top - verify descent options before committing

name: Rainbow Falls
access: Icicle Creek area (specific location requires local knowledge). Accessed via waterfall approach.
watch_out: Waterfall ice conditions highly variable - water flow changes affect ice quality hour-to-hour
Running water creates unpredictable thin sections and hollow ice - visual inspection essential
Creek crossing hazard on approach - water level affects accessibility
Water-spitting holes common at base of falls - avoid belaying directly below waterfall
Serac/icefall hazard: active waterfall means unstable ice blocks possible - timing critical
Difficult approach route-finding in winter - tricky navigation to base of flow
Limited descent options - evaluate retreat plan carefully before committing to climb
Seasonal variability: highly dependent on water flow and temperature patterns

name: Plastic Fantastic
access: Icicle Creek vicinity, exact location requires local beta
watch_out: Thin ice sections - requires careful footwork and ice tool precision
Running water hazard - active water flow creates unpredictable ice conditions
Avalanche approach hazard - access slopes can slide
South-facing aspect: sun-driven melting limits climbing window
Serac/icefall potential from adjacent ice formations
Route-finding difficulty in winter - requires current beta before attempting

name: Right of Hubba Hubba
access: Hubba Hubba area, Icicle Creek Road, 6-8 miles up canyon. Actually two separate climbs to the right of main Hubba Hubba flow.
watch_out: PG13 rating indicates serious hazards - run-out sections with limited protection
Always thin ice - most times cannot take screws above 10cm; severely limited protection options
Difficult screw placement requires small-diameter ice screws or creative anchor rigging
Two flows vary drastically by conditions - require mixed climbing and trad gear for route changes
Steeper than left side - more sustained climbing with higher commitment and serious consequence of falls
Fun but 'spicy' climbing - expert mixed ice and rock skills required
Rockfall hazard: steep rock sections above ice can produce falling debris after sun exposure
Approach avalanche danger: 30-45 degree slopes prone to soft slab avalanches
Icefall hazard: adjacent flows may shed ice - monitor for unstable formations
Wind-loaded terrain above: Christmas crust layer (25-35 cm at 4,500 ft) creates ongoing instability
Descent with limited anchor options - verify retreat strategy before committing
Raptor closure: Bridge Creek Wall area (1/2 mile buffer) closed January 1 - August 15 for golden eagle nesting
